name: Sumerian City-States
notes: Collapsed under Akkadian conquest; drought also implicated

name: Akkadian Empire
notes: 4.2 kiloyear climate event strongly implicated in collapse

name: Neo-Babylonian Empire
notes: Fell to Cyrus the Great of Persia

name: Achaemenid Persian Empire
notes: Conquered by Alexander the Great

name: Sassanid Empire
notes: Weakened by plague and Byzantine wars; fell to Islamic conquest

name: Ancient Egypt - New Kingdom
notes: Bronze Age Collapse; Sea Peoples invasions and internal fragmentation

name: Classical Greece
notes: Absorbed by Macedon then Rome; internal city-state rivalry weakened cohesion

name: Macedonian Empire
notes: Fragmented immediately on Alexander's death into Diadochi kingdoms

name: Roman Empire
notes: Western Rome fell 476 CE; overextension, migration pressure, economic collapse

name: Byzantine Empire
notes: Gradual erosion over centuries; final conquest by Ottomans 1453

name: Parthian Empire
notes: Rome's great eastern rival; fell to internal Sassanid rebellion

name: Umayyad Caliphate
notes: Overthrown by Abbasid Revolution; internal ethnic tensions

name: Abbasid Caliphate
notes: Sacked by Mongols 1258; Baghdad destroyed

name: Ottoman Empire
notes: Sick Man of Europe; WWI defeat triggered final collapse

name: Maurya Empire
notes: Fragmented after Ashoka; succession crises

name: Gupta Empire
notes: Huna invasions triggered fragmentation

name: Mughal Empire
notes: Long fragmentation post-Aurangzeb; British absorbed remnants 1857

name: Qin Dynasty
notes: Collapsed under peasant revolts after Qin Shi Huang's death

name: Han Dynasty
notes: Yellow Turban Rebellion; warlordism; Three Kingdoms period

name: Tang Dynasty
notes: An Lushan Rebellion weakened Tang; Huang Chao Rebellion finished it

name: Song Dynasty
notes: Conquered by Kublai Khan's Mongols

name: Yuan Dynasty
notes: Black Death devastated population; Red Turban Rebellion overthrew Mongol rule

name: Ming Dynasty
notes: Little Ice Age famines; peasant rebellions; Manchu conquest

name: Qing Dynasty
notes: Opium Wars, Taiping Rebellion, Boxer Rebellion; abdication 1912

name: Mongol Empire
notes: Largest contiguous empire; fragmented into khanates after 1259

name: Timurid Empire
notes: Tamerlane's empire; fragmented after his death; Babur fled to found Mughals

name: Kingdom of Kush
notes: Conquered by Aksum; desertification also weakened agricultural base

name: Kingdom of Aksum
notes: Islamic expansion cut Red Sea trade routes; climate shifts weakened agriculture

name: Mali Empire
notes: Fragmented as Songhai rose; succession disputes weakened central authority

name: Classic Maya
notes: Severe drought cycles; internal warfare; agricultural collapse

name: Teotihuacan
notes: Evidence of internal burning and destruction; drought likely contributing factor

name: Aztec Empire
notes: Spanish conquest aided by smallpox epidemic and local alliances

name: Inca Empire
notes: Smallpox preceded Spanish arrival; civil war weakened resistance

name: Carolingian Empire
notes: Treaty of Verdun 843 split empire; Viking pressure accelerated fragmentation

name: Holy Roman Empire
notes: 30 Years War devastated it; Napoleon dissolved it formally 1806

name: Spanish Empire
notes: Napoleonic invasion triggered Latin American independence movements

name: Napoleonic Empire
notes: Russian campaign overextension; 1815 Waterloo; volcanic winter 1815 (Tambora)

name: Austro-Hungarian Empire
notes: WWI defeat; multinational empire fragmented into nation-states

name: German Empire and Third Reich
notes: Two-front overextension; unconditional surrender 1945

name: Italian Colonial Empire
notes: WWII defeats in Africa and Europe; Allied invasion of Italy 1943

name: Empire of Japan
notes: Pacific overextension; atomic bombs; unconditional surrender 1945

name: French Colonial Empire
notes: Indochina War then Algeria triggered decolonisation wave

name: British Empire
notes: WWII economic exhaustion; Indian independence 1947 triggered decolonisation

name: Russian Empire and USSR
notes: Cold War economic exhaustion; Gorbachev reforms triggered rapid dissolution

name: Portuguese Empire
notes: Carnation Revolution 1974 triggered rapid decolonisation; Macau handover 1999

name: Indus Valley Civilisation
notes: Gradual urban abandonment; likely drought and migration from Aryans

name: Kushan Empire
notes: Junction of Rome, Parthia, China, India; fell to Sassanid pressure

name: Chola Dynasty
notes: Maritime empire; reached Southeast Asia; fell to Pandya and Hoysala

name: Delhi Sultanate
notes: Five successive dynasties; weakened by Mongol raids; fell to Babur

name: Vijayanagara Empire
notes: Destroyed at Battle of Talikota 1565; single catastrophic defeat

name: Maratha Empire
notes: Defeated at Panipat 1761 by Afghans; then absorbed by British

name: Ghana Empire
notes: Gold-salt trade empire; Almoravid invasion 1076 began decline

name: Kanem-Bornu Empire
notes: One of longest-lived African states; survived multiple crises and relocations

name: Kingdom of Kongo
notes: Battle of Mbwila 1665 devastated kingdom; gradual Portuguese absorption

name: Mutapa Empire
notes: Portuguese disruption of gold trade routes; gradual vassalisation

name: Ethiopian Empire
notes: Only African state to resist colonisation; fell to Haile Selassie coup 1974

name: Sokoto Caliphate
notes: Largest 19th century African state; fell to British Lugard expedition 1903

name: Kingdom of Benin
notes: British Punitive Expedition 1897; famous bronzes looted

name: Kilwa Sultanate
notes: East African maritime trade city-state; destroyed by Portuguese fleet 1505

name: Asante Empire
notes: Won three Anglo-Asante wars; fell in fourth 1896-1902

name: Hittite Empire
notes: Bronze Age Collapse; Sea Peoples and drought ended Hittite power

name: Mycenaean Greece
notes: Bronze Age Collapse; palaces destroyed, writing lost for centuries

name: Carthage
notes: Destroyed by Rome in Third Punic War; city razed, population enslaved

name: Seleucid Empire
notes: Largest Alexander successor state; lost territory to Parthia and Rome

name: Shang Dynasty
notes: First historically documented Chinese dynasty; oracle bones; fell to Zhou

name: Zhou Dynasty
notes: Longest Chinese dynasty; Warring States period ended it; Confucius era

name: Sui Dynasty
notes: Reunified China after centuries; overextension in Korea wars caused revolt

name: Khmer Empire
notes: Angkor Wat builders; prolonged drought and Ayutthaya raids ended empire

name: Srivijaya Empire
notes: Maritime trade empire; Chola raids 1025 CE weakened it decisively

name: Kievan Rus
notes: Mongol invasion 1237-1240 destroyed major cities including Kiev

name: Kingdom of France
notes: French Revolution 1789-1792 ended monarchy; gave way to Republic and Napoleon

name: Republic of Venice
notes: Napoleon dissolved it in 1797; traded to Austria in Treaty of Campo Formio

name: Xiongnu Confederation
notes: Han Dynasty's main steppe rival; forced Han to build Great Wall; split into northern and southern

name: Golden Horde
notes: Mongol successor; Black Death devastated it; fragmented into smaller khanates

name: Goryeo
notes: Gave Korea its name; survived Mongol conquest as vassal; internal revolt ended it

name: Silla
notes: United Korean peninsula 668 CE with Tang help; 992-year lifespan

name: Koguryo
notes: One of Three Kingdoms of Korea; defeated multiple Chinese invasions; fell to Tang-Silla alliance

name: Balhae
notes: Successor to Koguryo in Manchuria and northern Korea; destroyed by Khitan Liao

name: Yamato
notes: Early Japanese state; transformed into imperial Japan through Taika Reform 645 CE

name: Tokugawa Shogunate
notes: 250 years of isolation; Perry's arrival 1853 forced opening; Meiji Restoration ended it

name: Liao Dynasty
notes: Khitan empire; dominated northern China; destroyed by Jurchen Jin

name: Jin Empire
notes: Jurchen empire ruling northern China; destroyed by Genghis Khan's campaigns

name: Xixia
notes: Tangut state; Genghis Khan's last campaign; population nearly exterminated

name: Pagan Empire
notes: First Burmese empire; Mongol invasion 1277 devastated it; 10,000 temples built

name: Ayutthaya
notes: Thai kingdom; capital destroyed by Burmese 1767; one of richest cities in Asia

name: Champa
notes: Hindu-Buddhist maritime state; gradually absorbed by Vietnamese expansion southward

name: Dai Viet
notes: Survived three Mongol invasions; key resilience case; became French Indochina

name: Majapahit
notes: Last major Hindu empire in Southeast Asia; fragmented as Islam spread

name: Ilkhanate
notes: Mongol Persia; Black Death and succession crises ended it rapidly

name: Safavid Empire
notes: Established Shia Islam in Persia; Afghan invasion 1709 ended it

name: Fatimid Caliphate
notes: Ismaili Shia caliphate; Saladin ended it; founded Cairo

name: Seljuk Empire
notes: Triggered the Crusades by taking Jerusalem; fragmented into successor states

name: Chagatai Khanate
notes: Mongol successor in Central Asia; Black Death and internal divisions ended it

name: Ghaznavid Emirate
notes: First major Islamic empire in Afghanistan/India; Mahmud's raids into India famous

name: Almohad Caliphate
notes: Battle of Las Navas de Tolosa 1212 ended Almohad power in Iberia

name: Almoravid Dynasty
notes: Berber empire from Sahara; conquered Ghana Empire; replaced by Almohads

name: Ptolemaic Kingdom
notes: Alexander's Egyptian successor; Cleopatra's kingdom; ended with Actium 31 BCE

name: Visigothic Kingdom
notes: Ruled Iberia after Rome; Islamic invasion 711 CE conquered it in 10 years

name: Angevin Empire
notes: English-French empire of Henry II; lost French lands to Philip II of France

name: Great Moravia
notes: First major Slavic state; Magyar invasion ended it; gave rise to Czech and Slovak identity

name: Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth
contemporary_rivals: Russian Empire and USSR|Holy Roman Empire|Ottoman Empire
notes: Three partitions 1772-1795 by Russia, Prussia, Austria erased it from map

name: Teutonic Knights
notes: Battle of Grunwald 1410 ended Teutonic power; dissolved in Reformation

name: Kalmar Union
notes: Scandinavian union of Denmark, Norway, Sweden; Stockholm Bloodbath 1520 triggered Swedish revolt

name: Dahomey
contemporary_rivals: British Empire|French Colonial Empire|Kingdom of Benin
notes: Famous for female warriors (Agojie); French conquest 1892-1904

name: Buganda
notes: East African kingdom; became British protectorate 1894; formal abolition 1966

name: Funj Sultanate
notes: Sudanese sultanate; conquered by Muhammad Ali's Egypt 1821

name: Adal Sultanate
notes: Somali-Afar sultanate; nearly conquered Ethiopia; Portuguese intervention saved Ethiopia

name: Wadai Empire
notes: Central African sultanate; last independent sub-Saharan state to fall to France

name: Chimu Empire
notes: Largest pre-Inca state; Chan Chan city; conquered by Pachacuti's Inca

name: Toltec Empire
notes: Pre-Aztec central Mexican state; drought and internal conflict ended it

name: Olmec
notes: Mother culture of Mesoamerica; colossal heads; environmental degradation ended it

name: Mixtec Empire
notes: Famous for codices and goldwork; absorbed by Aztec then Spanish conquest
